text: Could you get it to me by Thursday?

I have asked her three times, and I have offered help.
I have been waiting for two weeks.
If we do not have it by Wednesday, we lose the sprint.
I would like you to call her director today.
Sophia, I need to involve you. This is a last resort. The integration team is blocking my sprint.
Before I do anything: what have you tried?
I have asked three times: the fifth, the ninth, the twelfth. I made the ask smaller, and I offered someone from my team to help write it.
And the cost? Give me a number, not a feeling.
The sprint starts Monday. If we do not have the page by Wednesday, we lose a week on the go-live.
What do you want from me? And do not say help.
One call to her director today. Ask for the endpoint list by Wednesday. Not a formal complaint about her, a request for a page.
She will hear about it in ten minutes. There will be tension. Have you thought about that?
Yes. I am telling her this afternoon, before you call. I want to be transparent, and I admit I should have escalated a week ago.
I need to involve you, and this is a last resort.
I have asked three times, and I offered help.
I am telling her before anybody calls anybody.

So you want me to call her director. Fine. But before I do, understand what you are asking for. The moment I call him, Camila will hear about it in ten minutes, and you will work with her for the next two years. So do it properly. First, tell me exactly what you have tried, with dates -- three attempts, an offer of help, a deadline she agreed to. Second, tell me the cost of not solving it, with a number and a date. Third, tell me exactly what you want me to do. Not "help me" -- what, precisely? And Diogo, one rule: you tell her, today, before I call anybody. If she hears it from her director first, you have made an enemy for life. Escalation is a last resort, and it is never a surprise.

Sophia, I need to involve you, and this is a last resort. Here is what I have tried. I asked Camila on the fifth, in writing. I nudged her on the ninth, and I made the ask smaller -- one page instead of the full document. On the twelfth she agreed to send it by Thursday, and I offered to send someone from my team to help her write it. Thursday came, and nothing came with it. I have had no answer since. The cost: my team cannot start the tests, the sprint starts Monday, and every day of waiting is a day on the go-live. Here is what I would like you to do: one call to her director, today, asking for the endpoint list by Wednesday. Not a complaint about her -- a request for the page. And I want to be transparent with you: I am telling Camila this afternoon that I am escalating, before you call anybody. I want the document. I do not want a war.

Diogo tells Sophia he needs to involve her, as a last resort. She asks what he has tried, and he lists three attempts, with dates. She asks for the cost, and he gives it: the sprint starts Monday. Then he makes a precise ask: one call to Camila's director today. Finally, he says he will tell Camila himself, this afternoon, before anybody calls.
